title: Connor Side Table
summary: A 2x2-framed side table with a slatted lower shelf and a three-board glued-up top, built almost entirely on pocket screws.
description:
Every joint in this table is a pocket screw, which makes it a good project for building real speed and confidence with a pocket hole jig before tackling something with mixed joinery. The one decision that actually matters is which face of each 2x2 gets the pocket holes — pick the least visible side, because the opposite face is what people see once the table's assembled and the holes don't fully disappear even with plugs.

The lower shelf's slat spacing is specified precisely (roughly 1-7/8" gaps) and worth actually measuring rather than eyeballing, since five slats with uneven gaps read as sloppy in a way a solid shelf wouldn't. Clamp the tabletop boards while the glue sets — pocket screws alone will pull the joint tight, but a clamp is what keeps the three boards perfectly flush across their tops while that happens.

tools.note: Keeps the three tabletop boards flush while the glue sets.

cutList.note: Adjust shorter if this looks too large for your space.

steps.title: Cut the parts
steps.body:
From the four 8 ft 2x2s, cut thirteen side and shelf pieces at 18-1/2" and four legs at 23-1/4". Five 18-1/2" pieces fit a 96" board, so the whole 2x2 list packs comfortably into the four boards supplied. From the 6 ft 1x8, cut three tabletop boards at 22-1/4" — that is 66-3/4" of a 72" board, so cut carefully and keep your kerf losses small.

Sort the thirteen 18-1/2" pieces before you drill a single pocket hole: four go into the two side frames, seven into the shelf (two outside pieces plus five slats), and the last two tie the base together. Stack the four legs and check their ends line up — mismatched legs give you a table that rocks.

steps.title: Assemble the two sides
steps.body:
Drill a pocket hole at each end of four of the 18-1/2" pieces, and pick the face carefully — pocket holes do not disappear even when plugged, so put them on whichever side will end up least visible. That single decision is the one that matters most in this whole build.

Screw two of those pieces between two 23-1/4" legs with 2" pocket screws to make one side frame, one rail up near the top and one lower down where the shelf will land; repeat for the second frame. Keep the legs' outside faces flush with the ends of the rails, and check each frame across the diagonals before the second screw goes in. The plan does not fix the rail heights, so mark both frames off the same layout and they will at least agree with each other.

steps.title: Assemble the shelf
steps.body:
Drill pocket holes in each end of the seven remaining 18-1/2" pieces, again choosing the least visible face on each. Two of the seven are the shelf's outside pieces and the other five are the slats.

Fasten the middle slat first with 2" pocket screws, centering it exactly between the two outside pieces, then work outward with two slats on each side holding a 1-7/8" gap between them. Five slats at 1-1/2" plus four 1-7/8" gaps spans 15", so there is a little left over at each end — measure every gap with a tape or a spacer block rather than eyeballing it. Five slats out of step read as sloppy in a way a solid shelf never would, which is exactly why the spacing is called out.

steps.title: Assemble the base
steps.body: Drill pocket holes in each end of the two remaining 18 1/2" boards. Assemble the table base by attaching the shelf, the two side frames, and these final two boards together using 2" pocket screws.

steps.title: Build the tabletop
steps.body: Drill three pocket holes in two of the three tabletop boards, adjusting your jig for the 3/4" material thickness. Screw and glue the three boards together edge to edge using 1-1/4" pocket screws, clamping the joints as you go to keep the top perfectly flush.

steps.title: Attach the top and finish
steps.body:
Center the glued-up top on the base. Three 7-1/4" boards make it 21-3/4" across and 22-1/4" long, over a base that is 21-1/2" square with the legs counted in — so the reveal is 1/8" on two sides and 3/8" on the other two. That is small enough that it has to be measured at all four edges before anything is fastened, not judged by eye.

Attach through pocket holes drilled up through the base's top rails with 1-1/4" pocket screws, so nothing shows on the tabletop. Set the finished table on a flat floor and confirm it does not rock, then sand the whole thing, easing the leg edges and the shelf slat arrises, and finish as you like.

images.alt: Small wooden side table with a slatted lower shelf and a three-board glued-up top
